sanskrit_devanagari:
क्लैब्यं मा स्म गमः पार्थ नैतत्त्वय्युपपद्यते ।
क्षुद्रं हृदयदौर्बल्यं त्यक्त्वोत्तिष्ठ परन्तप ॥ ३ ॥
sanskrit_iast:
klaibyaṁ mā sma gamaḥ pārtha naitat tvayy upapadyate |
kṣudraṁ hṛdaya-daurbalyaṁ tyaktvottiṣṭha paraṁtapa || 3 ||

sanskrit_sources.agreement: exact (raw HTML scrape; akṣara sequence identical)
exact (academic critical edition; Bhg_02.003 = MBh_06,024.003). Text body identical.

translations.verbatim_quote: O son of Pṛthā, do not yield to this degrading impotence. It does not become you. Give up such petty weakness of heart and arise, O chastiser of the enemy.
O Parth, it does not befit you to yield to this unmanliness. Give up such petty weakness of heart and arise, O vanquisher of enemies.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: Arjuna was addressed as the son of Pṛthā, who happened to be the sister of Kṛṣṇa's father Vasudeva. Therefore Arjuna had a blood relationship with Kṛṣṇa. If the son of a kṣatriya declines to fight, he is called a kṣatriya only in name, and similarly if a brāhmaṇa acts impiously he is a brāhmaṇa only in name.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya)
commentaries.copyright_holder: Advaita Ashrama, Kolkata (publisher of Swami Gambhirananda's English translation of Sankara-bhasya). No verbatim is captured because no commentary exists for this verse.
commentaries.notes: Site bhagavad-gita.us states explicitly that Sri Sankaracharya did not comment on this sloka and that his commentary starts from 2.10. Standard Advaita Ashrama (Gambhirananda) edition agrees - Sankara's bhasya begins at BG 2.10.
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Shankara reads klaibyam not as a slur on masculinity per se but as the loss of the active capacity that defines the kṣatriya role. The verse names two distinct things: the outward yielding to klaibyam, and the inward kṣudra-hṛdaya-daurbalyam (petty weakness of the heart). Both must be discarded; both are diagnosed as not-Arjuna ('naitat tvayy upapadyate' — 'this does not arise from your true nature').

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (per Sri Ramakrishna Math edition; bhagavad-gita.us reproduces this English rendering of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 2.1 – 2.3 Sanjaya said — Lord said — When Arjuna thus sat, the Lord, opposing his action, said: ‘What is the reason for your misplaced grief? Arise for battle, abandoning this grief, which has arisen in a critical situation, which can come only in men of wrong understanding, which is an obstacle for
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Ramanuja stresses that 'uttiṣṭha' (stand up) is the operational instruction; the prohibitions clear the field but the imperative is to act. The kṣudra-hṛdaya-daurbalyam is 'petty' precisely because it is disproportionate to the warrior's actual capacity — a weakness Arjuna can release because it is not constitutive of him.

positions.rendering: loss of the active capacity proper to a warrior

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: klaibyam literally references the state of a klība (a eunuch) and was a strong gendered term in the original cultural context. Modern translations vary on whether to preserve the gendered force ('unmanliness') or render it as functional impotence. Reading it as 'cowardice' is too soft; reading it as a slur is too hostile. The Shankara reading — loss of the active capacity definitive of one's role — preserves the bite without the gendered baggage.

literal_meaning: Do not yield to klaibyam (impotence/cowardice), Pārtha; it does not befit you. Casting off this petty weakness of heart, stand up, O scorcher of foes.
traditional_meaning_consensus: Krishna's second sharp line — and the one that contains the operative imperative: uttiṣṭha (stand up). The verse pairs a prohibition (do not yield to faintheartedness) with a positive command (stand up and act). Critically, the verse names the weakness as 'petty' and 'not constitutive of you' — Krishna is not condemning Arjuna as a person, he is rejecting Arjuna's collapse as inconsistent with who Arjuna actually is. The chapter's didactic voice is fully formed by this verse: clear, sharp, surgical. The teaching has moved from diagnosis (2.2) to direct correction (2.3).

remaining_gaps: Shankara verbatim — site states he did not comment on 2.3; bhasya begins at 2.10.
